title: COSTS OF NEGATIVE EQUITY
full_text:
Shrinking home values in most Australian cities have fuelled fears of borrowers sliding into negative equity and prompted warnings from mortgage specialists for people to examine their home loans and lenders.
Since the Commonwealth Bank forecast last month that Sydney prices will fall 6 per cent this year, Melbourne 7 per cent and Canberra 2 per cent, the market has worsened and borrowers are increasingly worrying about what happens when their asset value drops below their debt level.
The latest data from PropTrack shows home values fell in June in every capital city except Darwin, while crumbling confidence among buyers also has been reflected in home auction activity, which has sunk to its lowest level since the pandemic.
The federal government’s budget assault on landlords is tipped to further affect home values, with CBA forecasting investor lending to fall to about half its late-2025 levels.
While falling property values do not have as severe a financial impact on people who bought homes years ago and are sitting on windfall gains while paying down their mortgages, newer owner-occupiers and investors are increasingly concerned as they stare down the barrel of negative equity.
Homeowners looking to refinance might strike serious problems. Two Red Shoes mortgage broker Brett Sutton said questions from clients had recently changed from “how much can I borrow” to “what happens if values keep falling”. “That’s a significant psychological shift,” he said.
Mr Sutton said while negative equity – where a loan balance exceeds a property’s value – was not a crisis on its own, it became one as soon as something forced a sale, such as a job loss, relationship breakdown or unexpected health event.
For refinancing loans, negative equity “effectively closes the door”, he said. “Every refinance application is assessed against current market value not what you paid.
“Once your equity drops below 20 per cent, you trigger lenders mortgage insurance (LMI), which adds thousands to upfront costs and pushes you into a higher interest rate bracket entirely.
“If you’re sitting in genuine negative equity, most lenders won’t touch you at all.” Beyond Bank Australia chief member banking officer Sophie Scott-Young said negative equity reduced flexibility and narrowed options for borrowers, and could affect short-term plans such as renovations.
“The bigger impacts are often psychological – the feeling you are going backwards, even if you’re not,” she said. “Over the longer term, property values have historically recovered so if you can hold your position now it generally resolves with time.” Ms Scott-Young said regulator data showed most Australians were continuing to meet their mortgage repayments.
“Many of these have probably been prudent and built up a buffer in the previous low-rate environment while their property value was increasing,” she said.
Figures from credit reporting agency Experian show that about 51,000 Australian borrowers are at least one payment behind on their mortgage.
Experian head of analytics consulting and insights Louis Tsang said the current figure was about 16 per cent lower than two years ago.
“This suggests many consumers may have adjusted to the higher interest rate environment and associated repayment increases,” he said.
“Longer term, the arrears trend has not materially changed.
“One factor that may be contributing is the use of lender hardship programs which, through the data we observe, can put downward pressure on arrears figures.” Ms Scott-Young, who said she expected to see “flat to modest growth” in property prices this year, said borrowers should not react to paper losses in asset values. She said they should consider making small extra repayments, and use offset accounts to help “build a buffer against any change in value”.
Mr Sutton said borrowers facing negative equity should not panic-sell and should attack their loan balance to improve their loan-to-value ratio (LVR) and talk to a broker “before you hit a cliff”.
“Build a cash buffer,” he said. “The RBA’s own research is clear: the real default risk materialises when negative equity combines with a loss of income.
“Keeping even $10,000-$15,000 in offset provides a runway that prevents a short-term income shock becoming a forced sale at the worst possible time.” Ms Scott-Young said borrowers should be careful when choosing a lender “probably more than usual in the current environment”. “Take additional care in ensuring the lender is supportive and flexible when required,” she said. “Some organisations may become more rigid in the uncertainty regardless of the relation-ship with the borrower.” Mr Sutton said not all lenders responded the same way when valuations came in lower than expected or borrowers’ circumstances changed.
“In a rising market, almost any lender works,” he said.
“In a softening market, lender choice can be the difference between being financially trapped and having options.
“Some will work with you; others will simply serve you a letter. “For first-home buyers using the First Home Guarantee borrowing up to 95 per cent with no LMI, the lender they choose determines how the scheme is administered, what rate they land on and, critically, what their options are if the market moves against them. “The lowest rates advertised right now are almost exclusively reserved for borrowers with significant equity, typically at 60 per cent LVR or lower.”
